title: Why Decompose a Big Prompt
concept: Breaking a complex task into a sequence of smaller, focused prompts each with a narrow job.

exercises.prompt: Quick check: when giving instructions to a friend for a complicated task, which usually works better?

options.text: Give one long list of ten instructions all at once and hope they remember everything
Give one clear instruction at a time, checking in as you go
Give no instructions and let them guess what you want

exercises.explain: Giving one clear instruction at a time — and checking progress — helps avoid confusion and mistakes. This is exactly the idea behind prompt decomposition, which you'll dive into in this lesson!

cards.title: One Giant Ask, One Giant Mess
cards.body: Ever asked an AI to 'research this topic, write an essay, add citations, and make it funny' all at once? You often get a muddled result that's okay at everything but great at nothing.

cards.title: What Is Prompt Decomposition?
cards.body: Prompt decomposition means splitting one big, complicated task into a sequence of smaller prompts. Each smaller prompt has a single, narrow job, and its output can feed into the next prompt in the chain.

cards.title: Like a Recipe, Not a Miracle Dish
cards.body: You wouldn't tell a new chef 'make a five-course dinner' in one breath. You'd say: chop veggies, then boil pasta, then make sauce, then plate. Each step is small and clear, and the chef nails each one before moving on.

cards.title: Summarize-Then-Translate
cards.body: Instead of one prompt saying 'summarize this article and translate it to Spanish and format it as bullet points,' you chain three prompts: Prompt 1 summarizes, Prompt 2 translates that summary, Prompt 3 formats the translation into bullets. Each AI call has one job.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Prompts Are Smarter
cards.body: Truth: stuffing every instruction into one prompt doesn't make the AI 'think harder' — it often makes the AI lose track of details. Smaller, sequential prompts usually produce more accurate, more controllable results.

cards.title: Mega-Prompt vs. Prompt Chain
cards.body: Compare asking for everything at once versus asking for one thing at a time, in order.

left.text: 'Analyze, summarize, translate, and format this report perfectly in one shot' — often confusing, hard to debug, easy to mess up.

right.text: Prompt 1: analyze. Prompt 2: summarize. Prompt 3: translate. Prompt 4: format. Each step is simple and checkable.

exercises.prompt: Why break a complex task into a sequence of smaller prompts instead of one giant prompt?

options.text: Because smaller prompts cost nothing to run
Because each small prompt can focus on one narrow job, making results more accurate and easier to check
Because AI models can only read 5 words at a time
Because it guarantees the AI will never make mistakes

exercises.explain: Focused prompts let the AI concentrate on one clear job at a time, which reduces confusion and makes each step's output easier to verify before moving to the next step. It's not about cost or word limits — it's about clarity and control.

exercises.prompt: True or False: When you chain prompts, every single prompt in the chain should try to accomplish the entire task by itself, just in case earlier steps fail.
exercises.explain: False. The whole point of chaining is that each prompt handles only its own narrow piece of the task. If every prompt tried to do everything, you'd be back to one giant confusing prompt repeated multiple times, defeating the purpose of decomposition.

exercises.sentence: In prompt chaining, the output of one prompt often becomes the input for the next prompt in the sequence.
exercises.explain: That's the whole point of chaining: each prompt's output flows forward and becomes the input for the next focused step, like passing a baton in a relay race.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three task descriptions was most likely written by an AI trying to sound impressively 'all-in-one' rather than being a genuinely well-decomposed plan?

options.text: Step 1: Extract key dates from the document. Step 2: Sort dates chronologically. Step 3: Summarize events for each date.
This single mega-prompt will simultaneously analyze sentiment, extract entities, translate, summarize, fact-check, and generate a poem, all in perfect harmony in one seamless response.
Step 1: Identify the main topic. Step 2: Find supporting evidence for that topic. Step 3: Write a short conclusion.

exercises.explain: Option B is the giveaway — it promises way too much 'perfect harmony' from one single prompt doing six different jobs at once. That's a red flag for overpromising, unfocused AI output. The other two options show real decomposition: clear, narrow, sequential steps.

exercises.prompt: You're building an AI tool that reads a customer complaint email, figures out the sentiment, decides which department it belongs to, and drafts a reply. What's the BEST decomposition strategy?

options.text: One giant prompt that does sentiment analysis, routing, and reply drafting all in a single instruction block
Chain three focused prompts: (1) classify sentiment, (2) route to a department using that sentiment, (3) draft a reply using the sentiment and department
Ask the AI to guess the whole answer first, then never check its work
Skip sentiment and routing since the AI can 'figure it out' while writing the reply

exercises.explain: Chaining focused prompts wins because each step has one clear job and produces a clean, checkable output that feeds the next step. Classify sentiment first, use that result to pick a department, then use both pieces of info to draft a reply. This is exactly what prompt decomposition is for: breaking a tangled task into a sequence of small, dependable steps instead of hoping one mega-prompt magically juggles everything at once. That's decomposition in action — and you've now got the core idea locked in for this unit!
